scenarios.name: Engineering team planning — long conversation
scenarios.description: Extended team discussion across infra, frontend, backend, security, and deployment. Should trigger multiple message compactions and memory extraction.

conversation.content: Hey everyone. I've been evaluating databases for the new project. I think we should go with PostgreSQL for the main store and Redis for caching.
Great choices. PostgreSQL gives you strong ACID compliance and excellent JSON support. Redis is perfect for session caching and rate limiting.
Agreed on PostgreSQL. For the backend framework, I'd recommend FastAPI. We used it at my last company and it handles async really well. Our API deadline is April 15th.
FastAPI is a solid pick for async Python APIs. April 15th gives you about five weeks — should be doable for the core endpoints.
Perfect. I'll own the database schema and migrations. Bob, can you handle the API layer? Charlie will join next week to do the frontend in React.
Sounds like a clear division of work. Alice on DB, Bob on API, Charlie on React frontend. Make sure to align on the API contract before Charlie starts.
Will do. I'll draft the OpenAPI spec by Friday. Also, we decided all endpoints must require JWT auth — no exceptions. Token expiry is 15 minutes for access, 7 days for refresh.
Good security posture. JWT with short-lived access tokens and refresh rotation is industry standard. Make sure to store refresh tokens server-side.
One more thing — the staging server is at staging.internal.acme.com and the CI pipeline runs on GitHub Actions. Bob, please set up the workflow file.
Got it. GitHub Actions for CI, staging at staging.internal.acme.com. Bob will set up the workflow. Make sure to include linting, tests, and a coverage gate.
Already on it. I'm also adding Dependabot for dependency security scanning. Our minimum test coverage target is 80 percent.
Excellent. Dependabot plus an 80 percent coverage gate will keep the codebase healthy. You're well set up for a clean development cycle.
Let's also document that we're using Alembic for migrations and pytest for testing. These are non-negotiable standards for this project.
Noted — Alembic for migrations, pytest for testing. Having non-negotiable standards early prevents tooling debates later.
Last infra item: the production database will be hosted on AWS RDS, and we need to budget for a db.r6g.large instance. Alice, can you estimate storage needs?
AWS RDS with db.r6g.large is a good starting point. Alice should estimate based on expected row counts and index sizes for the first six months.
Hey team, I just joined! I'll be handling the frontend in React with TypeScript. What component library are we using?
Welcome Charlie! The team hasn't picked a component library yet. Popular options for React plus TypeScript are Radix UI, shadcn/ui, or Material UI.
Let's go with shadcn/ui — it's flexible and doesn't lock us into a heavy dependency. Charlie, also use Tailwind CSS for styling.
Good call. shadcn/ui with Tailwind gives you full control over the design system without pulling in a giant UI framework.
Perfect, I love Tailwind. I'll set up the frontend project with Vite as the bundler. Also, I'll use React Router v7 for navigation and TanStack Query for data fetching.
Vite, React Router v7, and TanStack Query is a modern and performant stack. Make sure to configure API base URL via environment variables.
Charlie, the API base URL for local dev is http://localhost:8000/api/v1. I'll add CORS middleware on the FastAPI side. Also, all API responses follow our standard envelope: {data, error, meta}.
Consistent response envelopes make frontend integration much smoother. The {data, error, meta} pattern is clean and predictable.

Quick update: I finished the initial database schema. We have five tables: users, refresh_tokens, projects, tasks, and audit_log. All use UUID primary keys and have created_at plus updated_at timestamps.
Five tables is a solid MVP schema. UUIDs and timestamps on every table is best practice. Make sure audit_log is append-only with no updates or deletes.
Nice work Alice. I've got the first three API endpoints ready: POST /auth/register, POST /auth/login, and POST /auth/refresh. All tested with 92 percent coverage.
92 percent coverage on auth endpoints is excellent. These three cover the core auth flow. Next up would be the CRUD endpoints for projects and tasks.

Frontend update: I've built the login page, registration page, and the main dashboard layout. Using React Hook Form for validation. The dashboard has a sidebar nav with Projects, Tasks, and Settings sections.
React Hook Form is great for performant forms. The dashboard layout with sidebar nav sounds well organized. Make sure the auth token refresh is handled transparently in the API client.
Team standup notes: we're on track for the April 15th deadline. Remaining work is projects CRUD, tasks CRUD, and the data export feature. Bob, how long for the remaining endpoints?
Good to hear you're on track. The remaining CRUD endpoints plus data export should be scoped carefully — export might need background job processing for large datasets.
I estimate three days for projects CRUD, two days for tasks CRUD, and three days for the export feature. Export will support CSV and JSON formats using a Celery worker with Redis as the broker.
Eight days total for the remaining backend work fits within the April 15th deadline. Celery plus Redis for async exports is the right approach for handling large datasets.
Agreed. I'll also set up the Celery worker config and the Redis connection. Our Redis instance is on AWS ElastiCache, endpoint is redis.internal.acme.com port 6379.
ElastiCache for Redis is reliable and low-latency within AWS. Make sure to use TLS for the connection and store the endpoint in environment variables, not in code.
I need the API contract for projects and tasks to start building the frontend CRUD pages. Bob, can you share the OpenAPI spec?
Getting the OpenAPI spec early is key for parallel frontend and backend work. Bob should publish it to a shared location or set up Swagger UI on the dev server.
Just pushed the OpenAPI spec to the repo at docs/openapi.yaml. Swagger UI is live at localhost:8000/docs. Charlie, you can generate TypeScript types from it using openapi-typescript.
openapi-typescript will keep your frontend types in sync with the API. Set it up as a build step so types auto-regenerate when the spec changes.
Security review finding: we need to add rate limiting to the auth endpoints. I propose 5 login attempts per minute per IP, and 10 registration attempts per hour per IP.
Those rate limits are reasonable. Use a sliding window algorithm backed by Redis. Also consider adding a CAPTCHA after 3 failed login attempts.
I'll implement rate limiting using slowapi library which integrates nicely with FastAPI. Storing counters in Redis. Also adding request ID middleware for tracing.
slowapi is a good choice for FastAPI rate limiting. Request ID middleware will be invaluable for debugging production issues across services.
Frontend is almost done. I added error boundary components, loading skeletons, and toast notifications for API errors. Using Zustand for global state management.
Zustand is lightweight and pairs well with TanStack Query. Error boundaries and loading states make the app feel polished. Good attention to UX.
Final architecture decision: we're deploying with Docker Compose for local dev and ECS Fargate for production. I'll write the Dockerfiles and compose config.
Docker Compose for dev and ECS Fargate for prod is a clean separation. Fargate removes the need to manage EC2 instances. Make sure health checks are configured properly.
Projects CRUD is done. Four endpoints: GET /projects, POST /projects, PUT /projects/:id, DELETE /projects/:id. All with proper validation and 88 percent coverage.
Four clean CRUD endpoints with 88 percent coverage. Good progress. Make sure DELETE is a soft-delete using the deleted_at pattern.
I added a project_members junction table for multi-user project access. Each project can have multiple members with roles: owner, editor, or viewer.
Junction table with role-based access is the right pattern. Make sure the owner role is required and cannot be removed from the last owner.
I built the project list page and project detail view. The list supports filtering by status and sorting by name or created date. Detail view shows tasks grouped by status.
Filtering plus sorting on the list page is essential for usability. Grouping tasks by status in the detail view gives a natural Kanban-like overview.
Tasks CRUD is also done. Same pattern as projects. Added a status field with values: todo, in_progress, done. Tasks belong to a project via project_id foreign key.
Clean task model with three statuses and project FK. Consider adding an assignee_id field later for task ownership tracking.
Good idea on assignee. I added assignee_id as a nullable FK to users. Also added a priority field with values: low, medium, high, urgent. And a due_date field.
Priority and due_date round out the task model nicely. The nullable assignee_id lets tasks exist without being assigned initially.
Export feature is ready. Two endpoints: POST /exports to start an async job, GET /exports/:id to check status and download. Celery processes the job in the background.
Async export with status polling is clean. Make sure the download link has a reasonable TTL and the exported files are cleaned up after 24 hours.
I built the export UI. Users select format CSV or JSON, choose date range filters, then click Export. A progress bar shows the job status via polling the GET endpoint every 2 seconds.
Progress bar with polling is good UX for async jobs. Consider adding a toast notification when the export completes so users can navigate away.
All migrations are up to date. We now have seven tables total: users, refresh_tokens, projects, project_members, tasks, exports, and audit_log. Running smoothly on RDS.
Seven tables is still a manageable schema. Good that migrations are current. Make sure to snapshot the RDS instance before any future schema changes.
Overall API coverage is now at 91 percent across all endpoints. I also added integration tests for the full auth flow and the export pipeline. Total test count is 147.
91 percent coverage and 147 tests is very solid for an MVP. Integration tests on auth and export catch the most critical user flows.

Team, we hit the April 15th deadline! Everything is deployed to staging. Let me list what we shipped: user auth with JWT, project CRUD with role-based access, task management with priorities, and async CSV/JSON exports.
Congratulations on hitting the deadline! That's a complete MVP feature set. Time to start user testing on staging before the production launch.
I'm writing end-to-end tests using Playwright. Already have 12 E2E tests covering registration, login, project creation, task management, and export download. Running headless in CI.
Playwright for E2E is excellent. 12 tests covering the core flows gives good confidence. Make sure to test edge cases like expired tokens and empty project lists.
Performance note: I added database connection pooling with min 5 and max 20 connections. Also added a health check endpoint at GET /health that returns DB and Redis status.
Connection pooling config is important for production. The health check endpoint covering both DB and Redis is essential for container orchestration liveness probes.

Monitoring setup: I configured Datadog for APM and log aggregation. Dashboard tracks request latency, error rates, and database query performance. Alert thresholds are p99 latency above 500ms and error rate above 1 percent.
Datadog for APM plus alerting on p99 latency and error rate is production-ready monitoring. Make sure to set up PagerDuty or Slack integration for the alerts.
Added Sentry for error tracking on both frontend and backend. Also configured structured logging with correlation IDs that match the request ID middleware we added earlier.
Sentry plus structured logging with correlation IDs gives full observability. You can now trace any error from the frontend through the API to the database query.

Last frontend item: I added a dark mode toggle that persists in localStorage. The theme uses CSS custom properties so it works with all our shadcn/ui components. Also added keyboard shortcuts: Ctrl+K for search, Ctrl+N for new task.
Dark mode with CSS custom properties is the cleanest approach. Keyboard shortcuts for power users are a nice touch. Make sure the shortcuts don't conflict with browser defaults.

scenarios.name: Product decisions with manual memories
scenarios.description: Shorter conversation with pre-loaded memories — tests that memories appear in assembled context.

manual_memories.content: Pricing model is freemium with $29/mo pro tier

conversation.content: Quick sync on the product roadmap. We're launching the MVP on June 1st with three core features: user auth, dashboard, and data export.
Three features for the MVP sounds focused. Auth is foundational, dashboard provides immediate value, and data export covers a common enterprise requirement.
I'll have the dashboard mockups ready by next Monday. The design system uses Tailwind CSS with our custom Acme theme tokens.
Great, Tailwind with custom theme tokens keeps the UI consistent. Having mockups by Monday gives engineering a full week head start.
One constraint: the export feature must support CSV and JSON formats. Excel can wait for v2. Also, exports should be async for large datasets — use a job queue.
CSV and JSON for v1, Excel deferred. Async exports via a job queue is the right call for large datasets — prevents request timeouts.

scenarios.name: Thread test — debug session
scenarios.description: Uses same source_id as scenario 1 — tests memories carry across. Spawns a focused debug thread.

spawn_message.content: I'm hitting a weird bug — the JWT refresh endpoint returns 401 even with a valid refresh token. Let me dig into this.

conversation.content: Found it. The token validation was comparing expiry as a string instead of a timestamp. Classic bug in jwt_utils.py line 42.
That's a common one. Make sure to parse the exp claim as a Unix timestamp integer, not a string comparison.
Fixed and added a regression test. I'm also adding type hints to the entire jwt_utils module to prevent this kind of thing in the future.
Good fix. Type hints on the token payload will catch this at lint time. Make sure the regression test covers both expired and valid tokens.
